verses.text: Balaam to Balak: build me seven altars, prepare seven oxen and seven rams. Balak did. Balaam: stand by thy offering, I will go meet the LORD. God met Balaam and put a word in his mouth: return to Balak, thus speak. Guidance: Pagan ritual at full strength — seven altars, seven bulls, seven rams. Balaam tries to leverage God like a divinity-for-hire. God meets him — and replaces his message before he opens his mouth. The volume of ritual cannot bend a holy word.

Balak brought me from Aram — come, curse Jacob, defy Israel. How shall I curse whom God hath not cursed? From the top of rocks I see him — the people shall dwell alone, not reckoned among nations. Who can count the dust of Jacob? Let me die the death of the righteous, let my last end be like his! Guidance: The man hired to curse pronounces a song that wraps Israel in glory. He even glimpses the death of the righteous and wishes it were his. Many envy the saint's end without choosing the saint's life.

Balak: what hast thou done? I took thee to curse, thou hast blessed altogether! Balaam: must I not speak what the LORD hath put in my mouth? Balak: come to another place, see only the utmost part, curse from thence. They built seven altars on Pisgah. Guidance: When the curse won't come, change the view. Balak thinks geography might do what theology won't. But God is not bound by elevations. The same word will come from every angle.

The LORD put a word in Balaam's mouth: return. Balaam: hear, Balak — God is not a man, that He should lie; neither the son of man, that He should repent. Hath He said, and shall He not do? I have received commandment to bless — I cannot reverse it. Guidance: The line that anchors a thousand sermons comes from the mouth of a corrupt prophet. God's faithfulness is so massive it forces truth from reluctant lips. A promise from Him is not a guess — it is a deed already done.

He hath not beheld iniquity in Jacob, nor perverseness in Israel. The LORD is with him; the shout of a king is among them. God brought them out of Egypt; he hath the strength of an unicorn. There is no enchantment against Jacob, no divination against Israel — What hath God wrought! The people shall rise as a great lion. Guidance: God sees His covenant people through covenant eyes — their sin atoned, their shouts royal, their strength terrifying. No magic works against a people God has decided to bless. The lion does not negotiate with the curser.

Balak: neither curse them at all, nor bless them at all! Balaam: told I not thee, all the LORD speaketh, that I must do? Balak: come to another place — peradventure it please God thou mayest curse from thence. He brought him to Peor that looketh toward Jeshimon. Built seven altars. Guidance: Balak's exasperation is human: just stop, if you can't curse. But Balaam knows he can only echo God's mouth. Third try, third site, third set of altars — the king of Moab still hasn't learned that holy will doesn't shop for venues.
